question.text: Under the PML Rules, for a company client, the "beneficial owner" is defined as a natural person who has a controlling ownership interest of more than:

answer.text: 10% of shares or capital or profits of the company
15% of shares or capital or profits of the company
25% of shares or capital or profits of the company
51% of shares or capital or profits of the company

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Under Rule 9(3)(a), for a company, the beneficial owner is the natural person who has "controlling ownership interest" meaning ownership of or entitlement to more than 10% of shares or capital or profits. For partnerships, the threshold is also more than 10%. For unincorporated associations, it is more than 15%.
**The Trap:** The 15% threshold applies to unincorporated associations or body of individuals — not companies. 25% is the threshold in some other jurisdictions (like the EU) but India uses 10% under PML Rules. Do not confuse jurisdiction-specific thresholds.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Beneficial ownership thresholds differ by entity type: Company = >10%, Partnership = >10%, Unincorporated association = >15%, Trust = beneficiaries with 10%+ interest. This granularity is tested in NISM XXIV.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** If no natural person meets the threshold, the senior managing official is deemed the beneficial owner. For entities listed on stock exchanges in India, beneficial owner identification of shareholders is not required — an important exemption.
